source_paper_title: Sample-Driven Optimal Stopping: From the Secretary Problem to the i.i.d. Prophet Inequality
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Correa_2024, title={Sample-Driven Optimal Stopping: From the Secretary Problem to the i.i.d. Prophet Inequality}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1363}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1363}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Correa, José and Cristi, Andrés and Epstein, Boris and Soto, José A.}, year={2024}, month=feb, pages={441–475} }
context:
Section 5.3, pages 23–24 (discussion around the limit as p → 1 and matroid secretary problem consequences)

An interesting question is whether something similar occurs for other classes of independence systems different than matroids of rank 1.

Proving that any of this problems behaves like the limit of p-DOS as p \to 1 on all matroids may be, in fact, a very difficult task.
title: Characterize limiting competitive ratio of p-sample matroid secretary via i.i.d. analogues
background_latex:
Let \((S,\mathcal I)\) be a matroid (or more generally an independence system) on a finite ground set \(S\). In a sampling-based online selection problem parameterized by \(p\in[0,1)\), each element \(e\in S\) is independently placed into an information set \(H\) with probability \(p\) and otherwise into an online set \(S\setminus H\). An adversary assigns nonnegative weights \(Y(e)\ge 0\) to all elements. The algorithm observes the elements in \(H\) (and their relative ranking by weight), then the online elements arrive in uniformly random order; upon each arrival, the algorithm must irrevocably decide whether to accept the element, maintaining that the accepted set remains independent in \((S,\mathcal I)\).

Let \(S[q]\) denote the random subset obtained by including each element of \(S\) independently with probability \(q\). The online set has the same distribution as \(S[1-p]\). For a fixed weight function \(Y\), define
\[\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,q,Y) := \mathbb E\big[\max\{\sum_{e\in I} Y(e): I\in\mathcal I,\ I\subseteq S[q]\}\big],\]
where the expectation is over the randomness of \(S[q]\).

For a class \(\mathcal C\) of independence systems, define \(\beta_{\mathcal C}(p)\) as the infimum, over \((S,\mathcal I)\in \mathcal C\), of the best competitive ratio achievable by any online algorithm in the above \(p\)-sampling model, where competitiveness is measured against \(\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,1-p,Y)\).

Let \(\mathcal M\) denote the class of all matroids. The matroid secretary conjecture asks whether \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(0)>0\), i.e., whether there exists a constant-competitive algorithm for the matroid secretary problem (the case \(p=0\)). The paper observes that understanding the limit \(L:=\lim_{p\to 1} \beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) and identifying an appropriate full-information/i.i.d.-type analogue whose optimal competitive ratio equals \(L\) would have major consequences.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a natural matroid-valued analogue of the single-choice i.i.d. prophet inequality whose optimal competitive ratio equals
\[L := \lim_{p\to 1} \beta_{\mathcal M}(p),\]
where \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) is the optimal worst-case competitive ratio for the \(p\)-sample-driven online selection problem on the class \(\mathcal M\) of all matroids (with adversarial nonnegative weights and random arrival order), benchmarked against \(\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,1-p,Y)\).

In particular, decide whether one can prove an equality of the form
\[\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p) = \rho,\]
where \(\rho\) is the optimal competitive ratio of a specified i.i.d.-type matroid online-selection model with known distributions (or another clearly defined probabilistic matroid model), analogous to how \(\lim_{p\to 1}\alpha(p)=\alpha^*\) holds in the rank-1 (single selection) case.
latex:

location_in_paper: Section 5.3, pages 23–24 (discussion around the limit as p → 1 and matroid secretary problem consequences)
open_problem_quote:
An interesting question is whether something similar occurs for other classes of independence systems different than matroids of rank 1.

Proving that any of this problems behaves like the limit of p-DOS as p \to 1 on all matroids may be, in fact, a very difficult task.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is about identifying/proving an equivalence between limiting competitive ratios across different probabilistic models on all matroids, not a finite falsifiable statement on bounded-size instances. Automated search over small matroids may generate hypotheses but cannot refute or confirm a universal limiting equivalence.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate matroid online algorithms or reductions and evaluate competitive ratios empirically on families of random/adversarial weight assignments for finite matroids, which provides a computable fitness signal. However, the core question is model-identification and asymptotic equality in the limit p→1 over all matroids, so computational experiments would be suggestive rather than decisive.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper formulates a p-sampling extension of secretary-type problems and, in the rank-1 case, proves that the optimal worst-case competitive ratio \(\alpha(p)=\beta(p)\) converges as \(p\to 1\) to \(\alpha^*\approx 0.745\), the optimal constant for the i.i.d. prophet inequality. In Section 5 it extends the model to general independence systems and matroids, defining \(\beta_{\mathcal C}(p)\) and highlighting the central open question: whether for \(\mathcal M\) (all matroids) the limit \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) can be characterized as the optimal ratio of a natural full-information/i.i.d.-type matroid analogue, in the same spirit as the rank-1 convergence to \(\alpha^*\).

Several candidate analogues already exist in the literature: the random-assignment matroid secretary model and prophet-secretary-type models on matroids. These models admit constant-competitive algorithms, so if one could prove that any such model has optimal ratio equal to \(L\), then \(L>0\) would follow. The source paper observes that this would immediately imply \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(0)>0\) via monotonicity and scaling relations in \(p\), thereby resolving the matroid secretary conjecture. This strong consequence suggests that either (i) the equality \(L=\rho\) for a known matroid i.i.d.-type model is very difficult, or (ii) the ‘correct’ analogue must be more subtle than existing ones.

At present there is no forward-citation evidence provided that the limit characterization has been proved, and the broader matroid secretary conjecture remains open with best known guarantees \(\beta_{\mathcal M_r}(0)=\Omega(1/\log\log r)\) for rank \(r\). Thus the problem remains open. Promising directions include: sharpening comparisons between p-sampling and stochastic matroid models (random-assignment, i.i.d. matroid prophet inequalities, prophet secretary), identifying obstructions (as in rank-1 prophet secretary upper bounds below \(\alpha^*\)), and developing structural/LP-limit techniques for matroid constraints analogous to the continuous LP and threshold structure that succeeds in rank 1.

sources.title: Matroids, secretary problems, and online mechanisms
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the matroid secretary problem (MSP) and initiates the study of constant-competitive online selection under matroid constraints with random arrival order.
sources.connection: The open problem asks about \,\(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for the p-sampling extension of MSP; this paper is the foundational definition and motivation for \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(0)\) (matroid secretary conjecture).

sources.title: Matroid Secretary Problem in the Random-Assignment Model
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies a stochastic variant of MSP where an adversary chooses values which are then randomly assigned to elements; gives constant-competitive algorithms in that model.
sources.connection: Section 5.3 of the source paper lists random-assignment MSP as a candidate i.i.d.-type analogue; comparing its optimal ratio to \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) is central to the characterization question.

sources.title: Prophet secretary for combinatorial auctions and matroids
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and analyzes prophet secretary problems for matroids (known distributions, random arrival order), giving constant-factor guarantees.
sources.connection: One of the natural i.i.d.-type matroid analogues mentioned in the source paper; the open problem asks whether such a model’s optimal ratio equals \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\).

sources.title: Prophet secretary through blind strategies
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops blind strategies for prophet secretary problems and proves upper bounds (including a 0.732 upper bound in the single-choice prophet secretary setting).
sources.connection: The source paper notes prophet secretary is unlikely to match the \(p\to 1\) limit even in rank 1 because of known upper bounds below \(\alpha^*\approx 0.745\); this informs what analogues cannot equal \(L\).

sources.title: O(log log Rank) Competitive Ratio for the Matroid Secretary Problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives an \(O(\log\log r)\)-competitive algorithm for MSP on rank-\(r\) matroids, implying \(\beta_{\mathcal M_r}(0)=\Omega(1/\log\log r)\).
sources.connection: Shows the best known guarantees for \(p=0\) on general matroids are far from constant; the open problem’s limit characterization would have major implications for the matroid secretary conjecture.

sources.title: A Simple O(log log(rank))-Competitive Algorithm for the Matroid Secretary Problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Simplifies and strengthens the \(O(\log\log r)\)-competitive approach for MSP, giving a clean algorithmic framework.
sources.connection: Provides the state-of-the-art for \(\beta_{\mathcal M_r}(0)\); underscores why identifying \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) via an i.i.d.-type analogue would be significant.

sources.title: The Secretary Problem with independent sampling
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies secretary-type selection with independent (binomial) sampling, relating it to dependent sampling and providing algorithms/analysis tools for the sampling model.
sources.connection: The open problem is posed in the independent sampling model for matroids; techniques for translating between independent and dependent sampling and analyzing sampling-based secretary problems are directly relevant.

sources.title: Competitive analysis with a sample and the Secretary Problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces/advances the secretary problem with a sample (dependent sampling), giving bounds and algorithms as a function of sampling rate.
sources.connection: This is a precursor to the source paper’s p-sampling framework; understanding the \(p\to 1\) limit for matroids is an extension of this line from rank 1 to general matroids.
